thread: Will Uther’s warning about Arthas matter before the prince burns too hot to stop?

evidence.clue: Uther tells Sylvanas that Arthas has never known how to step back from a fight once he thinks the field chose him.
The warning lands after Arthas has already mobilized, so it changes no immediate action but reframes all future consequences.
Uther backs Jaina’s smaller lane because he no longer believes warning alone will slow Arthas’s larger one.
Arthas hears the side plan, allows it, and still refuses to reduce the main column’s tempo, confirming Uther’s read of him in practice.
At Stratholme's gate, Uther stands present for Jaina's plain answer that the city cannot be saved cleanly, and Arthas responds not with widened judgment but with blank hardening.
Uther refuses the purge order publicly and leaves with every knight and soldier he can pull out of Arthas’s line.
Arthas ignores the refusal and proceeds with the Culling anyway, confirming that Uther’s warning was not enough to prevent the disaster it named.

readerKnows: The answer is no. Uther understood Arthas correctly, but that understanding did not prevent the Culling. It only made the break at the gate legible when it came.
povCharacterKnows: Uther and Sylvanas now know the warning was accurate and late in the only way that matters: it predicted the line Arthas would cross, but not in time to stop him crossing it.
notes: Resolved in chapter 17. Uther’s warning mattered as diagnosis, not as brake; by the time the truth arrived plainly, Arthas was already beyond stopping.
